FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD study (Data Release 4.0), three timepoints (baseline, 1-year, 2-year follow-up), N=8979, baseline mean age 9.5 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen time variables: TV watching, video streaming, video games, texting, video chatting (combined as online chat), social media. Applies B1 (YSTS paper with separate β for SM, texting, video chat, online activities)

FT_IC3.evidence: Graphical Vector Autoregressive Model (GVAR) temporal network with edge weights and coefficients; cross-sectional Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM); multiple inferential tests with edge weights and p-values reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Edge weights reported: TV-watching β=0.03, social media β=0.12, online chat β=-0.03 (temporal network, Figure 3 and text); cross-sectional edge weight for video streaming β=-0.03 (Year 2)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD analysis: three-wave longitudinal temporal network analysis with novel methodology

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2026 in Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology; no evidence of prior preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Fails condition (a): SM is NOT the dependent variable in every analysis. Paper reports SM → irritability (TV and social media predict increased irritability) AND bidirectional irritability ↔ online chat. SM is sometimes the IV (predictor of irritability outcome), violating the requirement that SM be DV-only. Applies B20 (bidirectional/cross-lagged).
notes: Bidirectional associations found between irritability and online chatting; unidirectional associations TV/social media → increased irritability. Results controlled for ADHD, anxiety, depression.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study, n=7043, baseline and 2-year follow-up data; US-based longitudinal study

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying screen time variables: passive watching (TV), gaming, social media, social engagement. Applies B1 (YSTS paper with separate coefficients for passive watching, gaming, social media)

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effect models with multiple coefficients and p-values; tests associations between modifiable lifestyle factors and cognitive development over time

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients reported in Tables 3 and 4: passive watching (various cognitive outcomes β=-0.22 to -0.40, p<0.001); social media (β=-1.40, p<0.001); social engagement (β=-0.50 to -0.90, p<0.001); F values and p-values throughout

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD longitudinal analysis of modifiable lifestyle factors and cognitive development; residual change score models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2026 in Journal of Adolescence; no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Fails condition (a): SM is NOT the dependent variable. Paper examines SM/screen time as PREDICTORS of cognitive outcomes (FCF and CCF), not SM as outcome. Cognitive development is the outcome, not SM.
notes: Sex moderates association between passive watching and inhibitory control/attention. Longer sleep and more nonphysical activities associated with greater cognitive increases; more passive watching and social media associated with lower cognitive increases.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study (Data Release 4.0), n=3,415 each test/validation sets (total ~6,830); US-based sample with longitudinal data

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time exposure included as one of six exploratory factors (weekday/weekend screen time). Qualifies as smartphone/screen time variable per B5 (general screen time measure). Factor 5 in exploratory factor analysis.

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression models examining relationship between risk groups (derived from factor analysis) and behavioral/brain outcomes; fMRI activation patterns analyzed; multiple statistical tests (β, t, p-values, confidence intervals)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients reported for risk group comparisons: BAS drive (β=-0.38, 95% CI -0.12, 0.64; p=0.04); fun seeking (β=0.46, p<0.01); various fMRI activation differences with t-values and p-values throughout

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD analysis: exploratory factor analysis identifying risk factors and regression models examining associations with behavioral and brain outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2025 in Journal of Adolescent Health; no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Fails condition (a): Screen time is not the dependent variable. Screen time is one of six predictor variables (alongside parental education, family income, prenatal alcohol exposure, parental aggression/ADHD, family violence) used to identify risk groups. The dependent variables are behavioral outcomes (BAS drive, fun seeking, behavioral problems) and brain activation patterns.
notes: Exploratory factor analysis identified six factors; screen time (weekday/weekend) was Factor 5. High-risk children (based on combined factor scores) showed altered reward function (reduced MFC/insula activation during reward anticipation, elevated impulsivity/behavioral problems). This is fundamentally a risk stratification study, not examining SM→outcome directionality.
